nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef; found among coral heads, soft corals (Sinularia), and associated with long-spined urchins at depths of 2–15 m.

diet.notes: Tends to accept meaty frozen and prepared foods including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, and high-quality marine pellets. May also take small live feeder shrimp. Nocturnal by nature, so feeding at lights-out tends to improve food acceptance.

avoidWith: very small ornamental shrimp (may be eaten)

summary: A small, nocturnal reef cardinalfish with a vivid red body and large black eye. Generally considered beginner-friendly; tends to do well in reef tanks and has been reported to accept frozen and prepared carnivore diets readily.
careNotes:
The Red Stop Light Cardinalfish is nocturnal in the wild, typically sheltering among coral heads and long-spined urchins during the day and hunting small crustaceans after dark. In captivity this behavior generally persists — feeding at or after lights-out tends to produce better food acceptance. It has been reported to form loose schools in the wild but can be kept singly or in groups in aquariums; groups tend to establish a loose hierarchy without significant aggression. Provide ample live rock with caves and overhangs for daytime refuge. While generally considered reef-safe, isolated accounts note that individuals may prey upon very small ornamental shrimp they can fit in their mouth.

Taxonomic note: the trade name Apogonichthys hyalinus is a junior synonym; the currently accepted scientific name per WoRMS (AphiaID 310871) and FishBase is Foa hyalina (Smith & Radcliffe, 1912). The species is also listed under the original combination Amia hyalina.

Temperature note: aquarium hobby sources consistently recommend 22–26 °C (72–78 °F), while FishBase field data for tropical wild specimens records 27.6–29.2 °C. Values here reflect an average of both; a mid-range of 24–27 °C is generally considered suitable for captive animals.
breedingNotes: FishBase records distinct pairing during courtship and spawning, and the species is a mouthbrooder, consistent with other Apogonidae. Captive breeding has not been well documented in the hobby literature reviewed.

additional.name: marinespecies.org (WoRMS)
additional.notes: Confirmed accepted scientific name as Foa hyalina; Apogonichthys hyalinus is listed as unaccepted synonym. Provided family (Apogonidae) and order classification.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.7 cm SL), temperature range (27.6–29.2 °C field data), habitat depth (2–15 m), order (Kurtiformes), mouthbrooder reproductive note, and IUCN Least Concern status. Temperature range is higher than hobby sources; averaged across sources for final values.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3 in / 7.6 cm), temperature (72–78 °F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), nocturnal behavior, and carnivore diet. Consistent with LiveAquaria on all aquarium parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Mouthbrooder with distinct pairing during courtship and spawning (per FishBase). Captive spawning is rarely reported; considered difficult in home aquariums.
